task_description: Construct a failure scenario connected to a specific Task 1.2 assumption

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying the strongest failure mode that closes a loop with Task 1.2 assumptions
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Which of my four Task 1.2 assumptions would produce the most dramatic and demonstrable failure if violated in an experiment? I want something that gives a clear, measurable performance collapse.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude suggested Assumption 1 (balanced dataset) because class imbalance is easy to construct artificially, produces a measurable and visually dramatic failure, and connects directly to a specific design choice in the paper (prior cancellation in Eq. 1→2). This confirmed my own inclination.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I independently verified this was the best choice by considering all four assumptions: Assumption 2 (AMT proxy) cannot be tested without new data collection, Assumption 3 (stable n-grams) would require LLM-generated text, Assumption 4 (positive only) would require negative fake reviews. Assumption 1 was the only one I could test with code alone on the existing dataset.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: N/A — the failure mode selection was my own decision.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Building the full failure mode notebook — scenario description, imbalanced dataset construction, experiment, visualisation, explanation
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Build the full Task 3.2 failure mode notebook. Scenario: 5.4% spam (8 messages), 94.6% ham (141 messages). Show that accuracy stays high (~94%) but F1 collapses to 0%. Explain why, link to Assumption 1 and Section 3.2. End with one-sentence fix using class_weight='balanced'.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude produced the complete notebook structure: scenario description citing Assumption 1 and Section 3.2, dataset construction code with replace=True for ham oversampling, cross_validate with four metrics, 2-panel visualisation (metric comparison + confusion matrix), and the failure explanation paragraph.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I ran all code cells and confirmed: (1) imbalanced CV gives F1=0%, recall=0% across all 5 folds while accuracy stays at 94.6%, (2) confusion matrix shows all predictions as ham (2 spam misclassified as ham, 0 spam correctly identified), (3) the majority-class baseline of 94.6% matches the imbalanced dataset ratio.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: Added the 'majority-class baseline accuracy' print in the dataset construction cell — I wanted this number explicitly stated so the reader understands that 94.6% accuracy is meaningless. Also extended the explanation paragraph to add the connection to Section 6 of the paper (authors acknowledge deployment on real platforms where class priors are unknown).

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Verifying that the suggested fix (class_weight='balanced') is technically correct and implementable
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Is class_weight='balanced' in LinearSVC the correct fix for the class imbalance failure? How does it work mechanically?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed that class_weight='balanced' scales hinge-loss penalties inversely proportional to class frequency, effectively giving each spam example 17.6x the weight of a ham example in this dataset, preventing the majority-class collapse. This let me write the fix sentence confidently.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified in scikit-learn documentation that LinearSVC supports class_weight='balanced' and that the weighting formula is n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y)), which for 8 spam / 141 ham gives the expected ratio.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: N/A — verification and one-sentence fix writing only.

top_5_prompts.prompt_summary: Build full Task 3.2 notebook: 5.4% spam scenario, F1 collapse demonstration, Assumption 1 link, fix
top_5_prompts.why_most_useful: Produced the complete failure mode notebook including the dramatic F1=0% result while accuracy stays at 94.6% — the empirical heart of Task 3.2.

top_5_prompts.prompt_summary: Which Task 1.2 assumption produces the most dramatic and measurable failure?
top_5_prompts.why_most_useful: Confirmed Assumption 1 was the right choice and provided the reasoning that became the opening of the scenario description.

top_5_prompts.prompt_summary: Is class_weight='balanced' the correct fix and how does it work mechanically?
top_5_prompts.why_most_useful: Verified the technical correctness of the suggested modification before committing to it in the notebook.

top_5_prompts.prompt_summary: Majority-class baseline calculation and why 94.6% accuracy is misleading
top_5_prompts.why_most_useful: The majority-class baseline concept was key to explaining why accuracy is a deceptive metric under imbalance — directly connecting to the paper's evaluation methodology critique.

top_5_prompts.prompt_summary: Section 6 connection: authors acknowledge unknown class priors in real deployment
top_5_prompts.why_most_useful: Grounding the failure mode in the paper's own Section 6 acknowledgement made the explanation substantially stronger.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Task 3.2 of Part B.
